title: Severe Anorexia Nervosa with Refeeding Syndrome on Day 2 of Hospitalization
subtitle: Start LOW (5–10 kcal/kg/day) + Go SLOW; THIAMINE 200–300 mg IV BEFORE Any Nutrition (Prevents Wernicke); HYPOPHOSPHATEMIA Is the Most Dangerous Electrolyte Shift; Replace Phos + K + Mg PROACTIVELY; Multidisciplinary Care

patient.name: L. Kovalenko
patient.code: Full Code (involuntary hold for medical/psychiatric stabilization; parents involved)
patient.admitDate: Day 2 of admission (admitted yesterday from outpatient eating disorder clinic for medical instability + suspected refeeding risk)
patient.room: Medical Behavioral Health Unit, Bed 12B (1:1 sitter for safety + meal supervision)

vitals.time: Day 2 0800 (CURRENT — 24 hr into refeeding; new fatigue + paresthesias)

labs.v: Height 168 cm, weight 39 kg → BMI 13.8 (SEVERE anorexia per DSM-5 criteria; extreme <15); weight loss 18 kg over 8 months (32% of body weight); ideal body weight ~58 kg; tricep skinfold + mid-arm circumference below 5th percentile; muscle wasting prominent (loss of temporalis + interosseous + thigh muscles); lanugo on back + arms (fine downy hair — sign of severe malnutrition)
labs.r: ANOREXIA NERVOSA severity (DSM-5): mild BMI ≥17, moderate 16–16.99, severe 15–15.99, EXTREME <15. This patient at 13.8 = extreme severity. Significant weight loss >20% body weight or BMI <15 = medical hospitalization criteria. Refeeding syndrome HIGH RISK at BMI <16 OR weight loss >15% in 3–6 months OR little/no intake >10 days OR abnormal pre-feeding electrolytes

labs.n: REFEEDING SYNDROME laboratory pattern (Day 2 of refeeding)
labs.v: Phosphorus (PHOS) 1.4 mg/dL (CRITICALLY LOW — normal 2.5–4.5; pre-refeeding was 2.8 — DROPPED with refeeding); Potassium 2.8 mEq/L (LOW — pre-refeeding 3.6; dropped); Magnesium 1.4 mg/dL (LOW — pre-refeeding 1.8); Glucose 132 (slightly elevated from carbohydrate refeeding + insulin response); thiamine level pending; albumin 2.6 (chronic malnutrition); calcium 8.2 (mildly low)
labs.r: REFEEDING SYNDROME = electrolyte + fluid shifts when malnourished patient resumes nutrition: insulin surge from carbohydrate intake drives PHOSPHATE, POTASSIUM, MAGNESIUM intracellularly → severe deficiencies; thiamine consumed by carbohydrate metabolism (deficiency → Wernicke); fluid retention (sodium/water shifts) → cardiac strain; CLASSIC ELECTROLYTE TRIAD: HYPOPHOSPHATEMIA (most dangerous + characteristic), hypokalemia, hypomagnesemia. Severe hypophosphatemia (<1.0–1.5) → cardiac failure, respiratory failure, hemolysis, rhabdomyolysis, seizure, coma, death

labs.v: Phos 1.4 mg/dL (CRITICALLY LOW — normal 2.5–4.5); SEVERE <1.0 mg/dL = imminent danger; symptoms emerging: NEW FATIGUE, NEW PARESTHESIAS, weakness, headache; cardiac monitoring ECG: prolonged QTc 478 (baseline 410), occasional PVCs (new); respiratory function reduced (RR 16 but shallow); STAT IV phosphate replacement initiated: SODIUM PHOSPHATE 15 mmol IV over 4 hr; recheck phos in 6 hr; oral phosphate supplement also added
labs.r: HYPOPHOSPHATEMIA in refeeding is the most dangerous + characteristic finding. PHOSPHATE is essential for ATP, 2,3-DPG (oxygen delivery), cellular membrane structure, neurologic function, cardiac contractility. SEVERE hypophosphatemia (<1.0) causes: cardiac failure (decreased contractility from ATP depletion), respiratory failure (diaphragm weakness), hemolysis (RBC ATP depletion), rhabdomyolysis (muscle ATP depletion), encephalopathy + seizures + coma. REPLACEMENT: oral if mild (>2.0); IV sodium or potassium phosphate if severe (<2.0); cap rate to avoid hypocalcemia + hyperphosphatemia + nephrocalcinosis

labs.v: K 2.8 mEq/L (LOW — normal 3.5–5.0); pre-refeeding K was 3.6; insulin-driven intracellular shift; KCl 40 mEq IV over 4 hr started + oral KCl 40 mEq PO BID; cardiac monitoring; recheck q4h
labs.r: REFEEDING HYPOKALEMIA mechanism: insulin surge drives K intracellularly. K depletion + cardiac risk (arrhythmias, prolonged QT, worsening). Replace gradually (IV peripheral up to 10 mEq/hr; central up to 20 mEq/hr in severe). Combination of low K + low Mg + low phos in refeeding = compounded cardiac risk

labs.v: Mg 1.4 mg/dL (LOW — normal 1.7–2.4); pre-refeeding 1.8; magnesium sulfate 2 g IV over 1 hr given + oral Mg oxide 400 mg PO BID; recheck q6h; target Mg >1.8
labs.r: HYPOMAGNESEMIA in refeeding from intracellular shift + chronic deficiency in malnutrition. Mg necessary for: parathyroid function (and thus calcium homeostasis), K cellular maintenance (low Mg makes it impossible to retain K — must replace Mg to retain replaced K), cardiac membrane stability. Replace IV for severe; oral if tolerable; balance against renal function

labs.v: THIAMINE 200 mg IV given AT ADMISSION (before any nutrition); continuing 200 mg IV daily x 3 days, then 100 mg PO daily x 30 days; never given any carbohydrate-containing IV fluid (D5W) before thiamine; if hypoglycemia or fluid needed and thiamine not yet given, use NS not D5
labs.r: THIAMINE DEFICIENCY in chronic malnutrition → WERNICKE ENCEPHALOPATHY (classic triad: confusion, ophthalmoplegia, ataxia) + KORSAKOFF SYNDROME (amnesia, confabulation) if untreated. CRITICAL RULE: THIAMINE BEFORE CARBOHYDRATES (and BEFORE ANY DEXTROSE-CONTAINING FLUID) — carbohydrate metabolism CONSUMES thiamine and can precipitate Wernicke if patient is thiamine-deficient. Standard prophylaxis 200–300 mg IV daily x 3–7 days at admission for refeeding-risk patients

labs.v: Day 1: started at 800 kcal/day (~20 kcal/kg/day for 39 kg = 780 kcal); 50% from carbohydrate, 30% protein, 20% fat; via oral diet + monitored meals (3 + 3 snacks); Day 2: advanced to 1000 kcal (slight increase but HOLDING because of refeeding labs); plan to advance 200–300 kcal/day if labs stable; goal 30–40 kcal/kg/day for weight gain (~1200–1600 kcal initially); target weight gain 0.5–1 kg/week; rapid advancement causes refeeding syndrome
labs.r: STANDARD REFEEDING APPROACH: 'START LOW + GO SLOW' — start at 5–10 kcal/kg/day for VERY high-risk patients (BMI <14, weight loss >20%) OR 10–20 kcal/kg/day for high-risk; advance 5–10 kcal/kg/day q2–3 days based on lab + clinical tolerance; if labs deteriorate, HOLD or REDUCE caloric advancement until labs stable; target 30–40 kcal/kg/day for weight gain in chronic phase

labs.v: Continuous cardiac monitoring (telemetry); 12-lead ECG on admission + daily: sinus bradycardia rate 56, QTc 478 (prolonged — from hypoK + hypoMg + chronic malnutrition + possible underlying QT), occasional PVCs (new from baseline), low-voltage T waves; no ventricular ectopy beyond PVCs; cardiac echo planned (assess for cardiomyopathy of malnutrition + pericardial effusion + mitral valve prolapse common in anorexia)
labs.r: CARDIAC complications of anorexia: BRADYCARDIA + HYPOTENSION (typical of severe restriction; should NOT be over-corrected with fluids — cardiac function adapted); QT PROLONGATION (electrolyte + chronic illness); CARDIOMYOPATHY (myocardial wasting); PERICARDIAL EFFUSION (rare; protein loss); MITRAL VALVE PROLAPSE (cardiac mass reduction); ARRHYTHMIA risk (torsades from QT prolongation); SUDDEN DEATH (major mortality cause in anorexia — ~10% of deaths)

labs.v: Medical hospitalization criteria for anorexia (per APA/SCOFF/MARSIPAN): BMI <15 (this patient 13.8); HR <40 or significant bradycardia (HR 44 admission); BP <80/50 or orthostatic intolerance (BP 84/52); hypothermia <35°C (35.8 — borderline); SEVERE ELECTROLYTE ABNORMALITIES (low phos, K, Mg); rapid weight loss; suicidality; failure of outpatient treatment; medical/psychiatric instability; this patient meets multiple criteria
labs.r: INPATIENT criteria for anorexia. Outpatient management primary mode; inpatient for severe medical instability + refeeding-syndrome risk + psychiatric needs. Specialized eating disorder units preferred when available. Multidisciplinary team essential

labs.v: Anorexia nervosa restricting type (no binge-purge); diagnosed 2 yr ago; multiple outpatient + IOP treatment attempts; current restriction pattern: ~500 kcal/day x 6 months despite outpatient treatment; intense fear of weight gain; distorted body image (states 'I'm fat' despite BMI 13.8); food restriction + excessive exercise; cognitive rigidity around food (counts every calorie, weighs food); social isolation; SECONDARY DEPRESSION; SI thoughts but no plan; comorbid OCD spectrum traits; family history mother with bulimia (recovered); college student (currently on medical leave); INVOLUNTARY HOLD initiated for medical stabilization (state-specific eating disorder commitment provisions)
labs.r: Anorexia nervosa subtypes: RESTRICTING (food restriction + excessive exercise) vs BINGE-PURGE (binge eating + compensatory behaviors). Comorbid psychiatric conditions common: depression (50–80%), anxiety, OCD, substance use. Family dynamics relevant (genetic + environmental). Multidisciplinary care: medical + psychiatric + nutrition + therapy (CBT, FBT for adolescents, DBT) + family. Recovery is long-term + recurrence common (~30%)

labs.v: Medical (internal medicine for refeeding/electrolyte management); psychiatry (eating disorder specialist, medications consideration for depression/anxiety); registered dietitian (meal planning + monitoring + advancement); psychotherapy (CBT/DBT/FBT depending on patient); social work (family dynamics + insurance + discharge planning); FAMILY-BASED TREATMENT (FBT) involvement (parents at bedside; agreed to support meal completion at home in eventual transition)
labs.r: Multidisciplinary care is the standard of care for anorexia. Each role essential. FBT particularly effective in adolescents + young adults (parents take charge of meals + weight restoration in early phase; gradual return of autonomy in later phases). FBT may be inpatient or outpatient. Family education + therapy essential

labs.v: SI present (passive — 'I wonder what would happen if I just disappeared') but no active plan; no intent; no access to means; 1:1 sitter at bedside for safety + meal supervision (prevents food hiding/disposal + safety + companionship); ANOREXIA itself is a slow-motion suicide attempt for some patients; mortality 5–10% per decade of illness (one of the highest of any psychiatric illness)
labs.r: Anorexia carries one of the highest mortality rates of any psychiatric illness — from medical complications + suicide. Routine SI screening + safety planning. 1:1 monitoring during refeeding (prevents disposal of food, exercising, purging). Restraints last resort. Document safety assessments q-shift

labs.v: CBC: WBC 4.2 (low — leukopenia of malnutrition), Hgb 11.4, Plt 138; BMP: Na 138, Cl 100, HCO3 24, BUN 18, Cr 0.6 (lean body mass low — Cr can be misleadingly low); LFTs: AST 64, ALT 78 (mildly elevated — refeeding hepatitis); TSH 1.8 (low-normal — sick euthyroid pattern), Free T4 0.7 (low); cortisol normal; FSH/LH low (hypothalamic suppression from low body weight); estradiol low (amenorrhea x 8 months)
labs.r: ANOREXIA LAB PATTERNS: leukopenia (marrow suppression), anemia (often mild), hypoglycemia tendency, low Cr (low muscle), elevated LFTs (starvation hepatitis or refeeding hepatitis), sick euthyroid (low T3/T4 with normal TSH = adaptive), HYPOTHALAMIC AMENORRHEA (low FSH/LH/estradiol), DEXA at admission shows osteoporosis (chronic estrogen deficiency)

labs.v: No fever; no overt cardiac arrhythmia beyond occasional PVCs; no respiratory distress; no obvious focal neuro deficit; no recent infection; no Wernicke encephalopathy signs (no confusion, no ophthalmoplegia, no ataxia — thiamine given prophylactically); no signs of refeeding-related congestive heart failure (no crackles, no JVP, no pitting edema beyond mild)
labs.r: Rules out concurrent complications. Wernicke prevention via prophylactic thiamine is working. Cardiac status acceptable but vigilant monitoring continued. Watch for refeeding-CHF: fluid retention from sodium/water shifts can cause volume overload in already-compromised hearts; cautious fluid management (avoid aggressive IV fluids; oral preferred when possible)

nursesNote: 0800 Day 2 — 19 y/o female with severe anorexia nervosa restricting type (diagnosed 2 yr ago, multiple prior outpatient/IOP attempts) admitted yesterday from outpatient eating disorder clinic for medical instability (BMI 13.8, HR 44, BP 84/52, hypothermia 35.8°C) + suspected refeeding syndrome risk. INVOLUNTARY MEDICAL/PSYCHIATRIC HOLD per state eating disorder commitment provisions; 1:1 sitter at bedside for safety + meal supervision. REFEEDING SYNDROME EMERGING on Day 2 (24 hr into refeeding at 800 kcal/day): Phos DROPPED from 2.8 pre-refeeding to 1.4 (CRITICAL — normal 2.5–4.5), K dropped from 3.6 to 2.8, Mg dropped from 1.8 to 1.4; new symptoms emerging — fatigue, paresthesias, weakness, headache; QTc prolonged to 478 (baseline 410) with occasional new PVCs. HYPOPHOSPHATEMIA is the cardinal feature + most dangerous (causes cardiac failure, respiratory failure, hemolysis, rhabdomyolysis, seizure, coma at severe <1.0 levels). REPLACEMENT INITIATED at 0700: SODIUM PHOSPHATE 15 mmol IV over 4 hr (with oral supplement) + KCl 40 mEq IV over 4 hr + magnesium sulfate 2 g IV over 1 hr; recheck phos in 6 hr, K + Mg q4h; cardiac monitoring continuous (telemetry); 12-lead ECG every shift. THIAMINE 200 mg IV given AT ADMISSION yesterday (BEFORE any nutrition) and continuing 200 mg IV daily x 3 days then 100 mg PO daily x 30 days (CRITICAL RULE: thiamine BEFORE carbohydrates — carbohydrate metabolism consumes thiamine + can precipitate Wernicke encephalopathy in deficient patient; never give D5W or any dextrose-containing fluid before thiamine in malnourished patient). Nutrition plan being HELD at 800–1000 kcal/day until labs stabilize (advancement plan was 200–300 kcal/day q2–3 days; goal 30–40 kcal/kg/day for weight gain; standard 'start low + go slow' approach — start at 10–20 kcal/kg/day for high-risk OR 5–10 kcal/kg/day for very high-risk BMI <14 + weight loss >20%); meals supervised by 1:1 sitter (prevents food hiding/disposal/exercising); 3 meals + 3 snacks; dietitian rounds daily. CARDIAC complications of anorexia present: bradycardia (HR 56 now, was 44), prolonged QTc (high mortality risk — torsades), low-voltage T waves, occasional PVCs (new from refeeding); echo planned to assess for cardiomyopathy of malnutrition, pericardial effusion, mitral valve prolapse (common in anorexia). SUDDEN CARDIAC DEATH is a major mortality cause in anorexia (~10% of deaths) — vigilant monitoring. SAFETY assessment: passive SI ('I wonder what would happen if I just disappeared') but no active plan, no access to means, no intent; 1:1 sitter for safety + meal supervision (anorexia itself = slow-motion suicide for some patients; mortality 5–10% per decade of illness — one of the highest of any psych illness); routine SI screening q-shift; document. MULTIDISCIPLINARY TEAM coordinated: internal medicine for refeeding/electrolyte management; psychiatry for eating disorder + comorbid depression/anxiety + SI; registered dietitian for meal planning + advancement + supervision; psychotherapy with CBT (cognitive behavioral therapy) + DBT (dialectical behavior therapy); social work for family dynamics + insurance + discharge planning; FAMILY-BASED TREATMENT (FBT) — parents at bedside (mother had bulimia, recovered — relevant family dynamic), engaged in education + agreed to support meal completion at home in eventual transition. ANTICIPATED COURSE: medical stabilization 1–2 weeks (electrolyte management + weight restoration initiation); transition to specialized eating disorder unit or intensive outpatient (IOP) when medically stable; long-term recovery is months-years with multidisciplinary support; recurrence rate ~30%. Family meeting with parents + psychiatry + nutrition + therapist + social work scheduled today to align on treatment plan, FBT principles, discharge planning, expectations. Mental health continues; depressed mood + anxiety likely respond as weight restored + nutrition normalized; SSRI consideration (fluoxetine has best evidence in anorexia — limited benefit; bupropion CONTRAINDICATED in eating disorders due to seizure risk; antipsychotics like olanzapine sometimes used for anxiety + small weight gain effect). Continue close monitoring of refeeding labs (PHOS + K + Mg + Ca + glucose + thiamine + LFTs) q12h; daily weights at consistent time (gown only, after voiding, before breakfast — standardize to detect true changes); cardiac monitoring continuous; respiratory function assessment (diaphragm weakness from hypophosphatemia is concerning); strict I&O.
providerOrders: ADMIT to Medical Behavioral Health Unit; INVOLUNTARY HOLD per state eating disorder commitment provisions; 1:1 sitter at bedside for safety + meal supervision

MULTIDISCIPLINARY TEAM: internal medicine (refeeding/electrolyte management primary), psychiatry (eating disorder + comorbid mood/anxiety + SI), registered dietitian (meal plan + supervision), psychotherapy (CBT/DBT/FBT), social work (family + insurance + discharge planning)

THIAMINE supplementation BEFORE any nutrition or dextrose-containing fluid: THIAMINE 200–300 mg IV daily x 3–7 days, then 100 mg PO daily x 30 days; CRITICAL RULE — thiamine PRECEDES carbohydrate administration (Wernicke prevention)
If hypoglycemia or fluid resuscitation needed before thiamine given: use NS or LR (NOT D5 or any dextrose-containing fluid)
REFEEDING NUTRITION PLAN — START LOW + GO SLOW:

Initial caloric target: 5–10 kcal/kg/day for VERY HIGH-RISK patients (BMI <14, weight loss >20%, prolonged starvation >10 days); 10–20 kcal/kg/day for HIGH-RISK patients (BMI 14–18, refeeding labs abnormal)
Advance 5–10 kcal/kg/day q2–3 days based on clinical + lab tolerance; goal 30–40 kcal/kg/day for sustained weight gain in chronic phase
If electrolytes deteriorate, HOLD or REDUCE caloric advancement until stable
Composition: ~50% carbohydrate, ~30% protein, ~20% fat (initially lower carbohydrate to reduce insulin surge; advance carbs as electrolytes stabilize)
Oral diet preferred over enteral over parenteral (gut > tube > IV); 3 meals + 3 snacks supervised by 1:1 sitter (prevents food hiding/disposal/exercising)
Target weight gain 0.5–1 kg/week (slow + steady; rapid gain causes refeeding + fluid overload + cardiac strain)
ELECTROLYTE management for refeeding syndrome:

PHOSPHATE (hypophosphatemia most dangerous): oral phosphate supplement (Phos-NaK or sodium/potassium phosphate) if mild (>2.0); IV phosphate for severe (<2.0) — SODIUM PHOSPHATE or POTASSIUM PHOSPHATE 15–30 mmol IV over 4–6 hr (rate cap to avoid hypocalcemia from phosphate-calcium binding); recheck phos q6h until stable + adequate
POTASSIUM (hypokalemia): oral KCl 40 mEq PO BID + IV KCl 10 mEq/hr peripheral (up to 20 mEq/hr central in severe); recheck q4h; target 4.0–4.5
MAGNESIUM (hypomagnesemia): IV MgSO4 2 g over 1 hr (severe) OR oral Mg oxide 400 mg PO BID; recheck q6h; target >1.8; MUST replace Mg to retain replaced K (low Mg prevents K retention)
CALCIUM (may drop with phosphate replacement): monitor ionized Ca; replace IV if symptomatic or critically low
GLUCOSE: BG q6h (insulin-mediated hypoglycemia rare but possible during refeeding; carbohydrate intake may cause mild hyperglycemia early)

Lab monitoring schedule: phos + K + Mg + Ca + glucose + BMP q6–12h x first week (depending on stability); thiamine level baseline; daily LFTs (refeeding hepatitis); weekly DEXA scan not needed inpatient (osteoporosis chronic)

Cardiac monitoring: CONTINUOUS telemetry x first week of refeeding; 12-lead ECG every shift then daily once stable; ECHO at admission to assess for cardiomyopathy of malnutrition + pericardial effusion + mitral valve prolapse; cardiology consultation if any concerning findings

CARDIAC complications to monitor: BRADYCARDIA (HR <40 = severe; do NOT over-correct with fluids — chronic adaptation), HYPOTENSION (do NOT over-resuscitate — risk of refeeding CHF), QT PROLONGATION (electrolyte + chronic; torsades risk if very prolonged or with QT-prolonging drugs), CARDIOMYOPATHY (myocardial wasting; improves with weight restoration), PERICARDIAL EFFUSION (rare), MITRAL VALVE PROLAPSE (common), ARRHYTHMIA, SUDDEN CARDIAC DEATH (major mortality cause)

CAUTIOUS FLUID management: AVOID aggressive IV fluids (refeeding fluid retention + cardiomyopathy = CHF risk); oral fluids preferred; if IV needed, NS not D5W until thiamine given; reassess daily; daily weights at consistent time (gown only, after voiding, before breakfast — standardize)

SAFETY: 1:1 sitter at bedside (prevents food hiding/disposal/exercising + safety + companionship); routine SI/HI assessment q-shift; remove access to means; suicide-safe environment; mandatory eating disorder hold per state provisions; psychotherapy continues

PSYCHOTHERAPY + MEDICATIONS: CBT + DBT + FBT (family-based therapy — especially effective in adolescents + young adults; parents take charge of meals + weight restoration in early phase); medications: FLUOXETINE (best evidence in anorexia among SSRIs — limited benefit; consider for comorbid depression/OCD); OLANZAPINE (low-dose; some evidence for anxiety + small weight gain effect — atypical antipsychotic, monitor metabolic effects); ⚠️ BUPROPION CONTRAINDICATED in eating disorders (seizure risk in eating disorder patients with electrolyte derangement + low body weight)

FAMILY-BASED TREATMENT (FBT): parents at bedside; family meeting today with psychiatry + nutrition + therapist + social work to align on treatment plan, FBT principles (parents take charge of meals + weight restoration; gradual return of autonomy in later phases), discharge planning, expectations; mother had bulimia (recovered) — relevant family dynamic

Long-term care planning: medical stabilization 1–2 weeks → transition to specialized eating disorder unit (ED unit) or intensive outpatient program (IOP); long-term recovery months-years; recurrence ~30%; multidisciplinary follow-up: medical/PCP, psychiatry, dietitian, psychotherapy; medical alert ID with eating disorder history; family education; peer support resources

Discharge criteria: medical stability (electrolytes normal x 48 hr off IV repletion; HR ≥45 sustained; BP ≥90/60; weight gain 0.5–1 kg/week); psychiatric stability + outpatient plan; family engagement + agreement to FBT principles; close outpatient follow-up scheduled (within 1 week of discharge); plan for ongoing meal supervision at home (parental supervision per FBT)

steps.title: Recognize Cues
steps.inst: Select ALL findings that are clinically significant cues requiring immediate nursing attention.

opts.text: HYPOPHOSPHATEMIA 1.4 mg/dL (CRITICAL — dropped from 2.8 pre-refeeding); new fatigue + paresthesias + weakness + prolonged QTc 478 + PVCs — refeeding syndrome with cardiac/neuromuscular manifestations
opts.rat: PRIORITY CUE — most dangerous refeeding manifestation. Severe hypophosphatemia (<1.0) causes cardiac failure, respiratory failure, hemolysis, rhabdo, seizures, coma. STAT IV phosphate replacement. Recheck q6h. Cardiac monitoring throughout.

opts.text: HYPOKALEMIA K 2.8 + HYPOMAGNESEMIA Mg 1.4 — completing refeeding electrolyte triad; cardiac arrhythmia + QT prolongation + torsades risk; replace IV (KCl + MgSO4) and oral
opts.rat: PRIORITY CUE — additional refeeding electrolyte shifts. K + Mg + Phos all drop in refeeding. Replace gradually. Must replace Mg to retain replaced K. Continuous cardiac monitoring.

opts.text: Severe anorexia BMI 13.8 (extreme per DSM-5); high refeeding risk (BMI <14 + weight loss 32% + prolonged restriction); medical hospitalization criteria met
opts.rat: PRIORITY CUE — severity stratification. Severity drives intensity of monitoring + refeeding caution. Multiple criteria met for inpatient admission. Multidisciplinary team essential.

opts.text: THIAMINE 200 mg IV given BEFORE any nutrition or dextrose; continuing prophylaxis x 3–7 days; CRITICAL RULE prevents Wernicke encephalopathy
opts.rat: PRIORITY CUE — Wernicke prevention. Carbohydrate metabolism consumes thiamine; refeeding without thiamine = precipitates Wernicke (confusion, ophthalmoplegia, ataxia) → Korsakoff (amnesia, confabulation) if untreated. Standard prophylaxis 200–300 mg IV daily x 3–7 days.

opts.text: START LOW + GO SLOW refeeding approach: 5–10 kcal/kg/day for very high-risk (BMI <14, weight loss >20%) OR 10–20 kcal/kg/day for high-risk; advance 5–10 kcal/kg/day q2–3 days; HOLD or REDUCE if labs deteriorate
opts.rat: PRIORITY CUE — refeeding strategy. Rapid advancement causes refeeding syndrome. Start at low caloric target; advance slowly with lab monitoring. Hold advancement if electrolytes drop. Goal 30–40 kcal/kg/day for weight gain in chronic phase.

opts.text: Cardiac monitoring: bradycardia HR 56 (was 44 admission), prolonged QTc 478, occasional PVCs (new); sudden cardiac death is major mortality cause in anorexia (~10% of deaths); echo planned
opts.rat: PRIORITY CUE — cardiac complications surveillance. Continuous telemetry x first week of refeeding. Echo for cardiomyopathy + pericardial effusion + mitral valve prolapse (common in anorexia). Avoid QT-prolonging meds. Cardiology if concerning findings.

opts.text: Anorexia mortality 5–10% per decade — one of highest of any psychiatric illness; from medical complications + suicide; passive SI present (no active plan); 1:1 sitter for safety + meal supervision
opts.rat: PRIORITY CUE — mortality risk + safety. Continuous 1:1 for safety + meal supervision (prevents food hiding/disposal/exercising). Routine SI screening. Anorexia itself is slow-motion suicide for some — extra vigilance.

opts.text: Multidisciplinary team: medical + psychiatry + dietitian + psychotherapy (CBT/DBT/FBT) + social work + family engagement (FBT particularly effective)
opts.rat: PRIORITY CUE — standard of care. Each discipline essential. FBT (family-based therapy) especially effective in adolescents + young adults (parents take charge of meals + weight restoration). Long-term recovery requires sustained multidisciplinary support.

opts.text: CAUTIOUS fluid management: AVOID aggressive IV fluids (refeeding fluid retention + cardiomyopathy = CHF risk); oral preferred; NS not D5W if IV needed (before thiamine)
opts.rat: PRIORITY CUE — fluid management nuance. The adapted bradycardia + hypotension should NOT be aggressively corrected (chronic adaptation; over-correction → CHF in cardiomyopathy + fluid retention from sodium/water shifts). Oral over IV when possible.

opts.text: MEDICATION SAFETY: BUPROPION CONTRAINDICATED in eating disorders (seizure risk in low body weight + electrolyte derangement); FLUOXETINE has best evidence (limited); OLANZAPINE low-dose for anxiety + small weight effect; AVOID QT-prolonging meds
opts.rat: PRIORITY CUE — psychotropic medication safety in eating disorders. Bupropion contraindicated. Fluoxetine for comorbid depression/OCD. Olanzapine low-dose option. Avoid QT-prolonging combinations (already prolonged from chronic + electrolyte derangement).

opts.text: Allergies: NKDA
opts.rat: Reassuring — no documented allergies. Document and proceed.

opts.text: Temperature 36.2°C (mild hypothermia — improved from 35.8°C on admission)
opts.rat: Mild residual hypothermia from chronic malnutrition; improving with refeeding + warming measures. Continue passive warming (warm blankets, room temperature). Not driving immediate action; monitor.

steps.title: Analyze Cues
steps.inst: Select ALL accurate linkages between cues and underlying pathophysiology / refeeding principles.

opts.text: REFEEDING SYNDROME = electrolyte + fluid shifts when malnourished patient resumes nutrition: insulin surge from CARBOHYDRATE intake drives PHOSPHATE, POTASSIUM, MAGNESIUM intracellularly → severe deficiencies; thiamine consumed by carbohydrate metabolism (deficiency → Wernicke); fluid retention (sodium/water shifts) → cardiac strain
opts.rat: CORRECT — central mechanism. Carbohydrate intake triggers insulin → intracellular shifts. The total body stores were depleted during starvation; the serum levels held up but now drop sharply with intracellular shift. Time course: typically days 1–4 of refeeding.

opts.text: HYPOPHOSPHATEMIA is the cardinal feature + most dangerous; SEVERE <1.0 mg/dL = cardiac failure (ATP depletion → decreased contractility), respiratory failure (diaphragm weakness), hemolysis (RBC ATP depletion), rhabdomyolysis (muscle ATP depletion), encephalopathy + seizures + coma + death
opts.rat: CORRECT — defines the danger. Phosphate is essential for ATP, 2,3-DPG, cellular membrane structure, neurologic function. Replacement: oral if mild; IV sodium/potassium phosphate for severe; cap rate (hypocalcemia, hyperphosphatemia, nephrocalcinosis risk).

opts.text: REFEEDING RISK STRATIFICATION (NICE 2006): HIGH risk if BMI <16 OR weight loss >15% in 3–6 mo OR little/no intake >10 days OR abnormal pre-feeding electrolytes; VERY HIGH risk if BMI <14 OR weight loss >20% OR prolonged starvation >15 days OR multiple risk factors
opts.rat: CORRECT — risk stratification drives refeeding strategy. Higher risk → more cautious initial caloric target (5–10 kcal/kg/day vs 10–20). This patient at BMI 13.8 + 32% weight loss + 6+ months severe restriction = very high risk.

opts.text: REFEEDING APPROACH: START LOW + GO SLOW; initial 5–10 kcal/kg/day (very high risk) or 10–20 kcal/kg/day (high risk); advance 5–10 kcal/kg/day q2–3 days based on tolerance; goal 30–40 kcal/kg/day for weight gain; HOLD or REDUCE if electrolytes deteriorate
opts.rat: CORRECT — standardized approach. Slow advancement is the key to prevention. Lab monitoring guides advancement. Carbohydrate proportion initially lower (50%) advancing as electrolytes stabilize. Oral diet preferred (gut > enteral > parenteral). Target weight gain 0.5–1 kg/week.

opts.text: THIAMINE 200–300 mg IV daily x 3–7 days BEFORE any nutrition or dextrose; CRITICAL RULE — carbohydrate metabolism CONSUMES thiamine; refeeding without thiamine = precipitates WERNICKE ENCEPHALOPATHY (confusion, ophthalmoplegia, ataxia) → KORSAKOFF syndrome (amnesia, confabulation) if untreated
opts.rat: CORRECT — Wernicke prevention rule. Standard prophylaxis at admission for refeeding-risk patients. Continue oral thiamine 100 mg PO daily x 30 days. NEVER give D5W or any dextrose-containing fluid before thiamine in malnourished patient — use NS or LR if fluid needed.

opts.text: CARDIAC complications of anorexia: BRADYCARDIA + HYPOTENSION (chronic adaptation — do NOT over-correct with fluids), QT PROLONGATION (electrolyte + chronic; torsades risk), CARDIOMYOPATHY (myocardial wasting), PERICARDIAL EFFUSION (rare; protein loss), MITRAL VALVE PROLAPSE (common — cardiac mass reduction), SUDDEN CARDIAC DEATH (~10% of mortality)
opts.rat: CORRECT — cardiac complications spectrum. Continuous telemetry during refeeding. Echo at admission for structural assessment. Avoid QT-prolonging medications. Cardiology consultation for concerning findings.

opts.text: MULTIDISCIPLINARY CARE for anorexia: MEDICAL (refeeding + electrolyte management), PSYCHIATRY (eating disorder + comorbid mood/anxiety + SI), REGISTERED DIETITIAN (meal plan + supervision + advancement), PSYCHOTHERAPY (CBT, DBT, FBT — family-based therapy especially effective in adolescents/young adults), SOCIAL WORK (family + insurance + discharge planning)
opts.rat: CORRECT — standard of care. Long-term recovery requires sustained multidisciplinary support. FBT (Maudsley model) particularly effective: parents take charge of meals + weight restoration in early phase; gradual return of autonomy in later phases. Family education + therapy.

opts.text: BUPROPION (Wellbutrin) is the first-line antidepressant for comorbid depression in anorexia
opts.rat: ⚠️ WRONG — BUPROPION CONTRAINDICATED in eating disorders due to SEIZURE RISK (low body weight + electrolyte derangement + bupropion lowers seizure threshold). FLUOXETINE (Prozac) has the best evidence among SSRIs in anorexia though benefit is modest. Olanzapine (atypical antipsychotic) sometimes used low-dose for anxiety + small weight gain effect; monitor metabolic effects.

opts.text: Aggressive IV fluid resuscitation with D5W to address the bradycardia and hypotension immediately
opts.rat: ⚠️ WRONG on multiple counts: (1) the bradycardia + hypotension are chronic adaptations — do NOT aggressively correct (risk of refeeding CHF from fluid retention + cardiomyopathy); (2) D5W BEFORE thiamine in malnourished patient = precipitates Wernicke encephalopathy (carbohydrate metabolism consumes thiamine — if any IV fluid needed before thiamine, use NS or LR).

opts.text: Advance caloric intake rapidly (500–1000 kcal/day per day) to achieve weight gain quickly
opts.rat: ⚠️ DANGEROUSLY WRONG. RAPID advancement causes severe refeeding syndrome. Standard approach is START LOW + GO SLOW (5–10 or 10–20 kcal/kg/day initial; advance 5–10 kcal/kg/day q2–3 days). Target weight gain 0.5–1 kg/week is appropriate. Faster gain = refeeding + fluid overload + cardiac strain.

steps.title: Prioritize Hypotheses
steps.sub: Rank nursing concerns from highest to lowest priority
steps.inst: Rank these nursing concerns from #1 (HIGHEST priority) to #5 (LOWEST).

opts.text: Refeeding Syndrome with Electrolyte Derangement (phos 1.4, K 2.8, Mg 1.4) r/t insulin-driven intracellular shifts with carbohydrate refeeding; cardiac/respiratory/neuromuscular complication risk
opts.rat: #1 — HIGHEST. The refeeding syndrome is the imminent danger. Hypophosphatemia is the cardinal + most dangerous feature. Replace IV + oral; hold/reduce caloric advancement; recheck labs q6h; continuous cardiac monitoring.

opts.text: Risk for Cardiac Arrhythmia / Sudden Cardiac Death r/t anorexia cardiomyopathy + QT prolongation + electrolyte derangement
opts.rat: #2 — HIGH. Sudden cardiac death is major mortality cause in anorexia (~10%). Continuous telemetry x first week. Replace electrolytes. Avoid QT-prolonging medications. Echo for structural assessment. Cardiology for concerning findings.

opts.text: Risk for Wernicke Encephalopathy r/t carbohydrate refeeding without adequate thiamine; thiamine consumed by carbohydrate metabolism
opts.rat: #3 — HIGH. Wernicke prevention via thiamine 200–300 mg IV BEFORE any nutrition or dextrose. Continue 3–7 days. Watch for confusion, ophthalmoplegia, ataxia. Korsakoff if untreated (amnesia, confabulation — often irreversible).

opts.text: Risk for Suicide / Continued Restrictive Eating r/t severe anorexia (mortality 5–10% per decade); passive SI; food hiding/disposal/exercising risk
opts.rat: #4 — Significant. Anorexia is slow-motion suicide for some. 1:1 sitter for safety + meal supervision. Routine SI screening. Psychiatric care + medications (fluoxetine, olanzapine — AVOID bupropion). Long-term recovery.

opts.text: Family Dynamics / Multidisciplinary Care Coordination r/t need for sustained team-based recovery support
opts.rat: #5 — Important but lowest in immediate physiologic stack. FBT (family-based therapy) especially effective in young adults. Multidisciplinary care: medical + psychiatry + dietitian + therapy + social work. Long-term outpatient structure essential.

steps.title: Generate Solutions
steps.inst: For EACH intervention, classify as INDICATED or NOT INDICATED for this client at this moment.

opts.text: STAT IV phosphate (15 mmol sodium phosphate over 4 hr) + IV KCl (40 mEq over 4 hr) + IV magnesium sulfate (2 g over 1 hr); oral supplements added; recheck phos q6h, K + Mg q4h
opts.rat: Refeeding syndrome electrolyte replacement. Phosphate is most dangerous + needs IV in severe (<2.0). K + Mg replaced with monitoring. Cardiac monitoring throughout (electrolytes + arrhythmia risk).

opts.text: THIAMINE 200 mg IV daily x 3–7 days (already started at admission BEFORE any nutrition); continuing prophylaxis to prevent Wernicke encephalopathy
opts.rat: Wernicke prevention rule. Carbohydrate metabolism consumes thiamine; refeeding without thiamine = precipitates Wernicke. Standard prophylaxis 200–300 mg IV daily x 3–7 days. Continue oral 100 mg daily x 30 days.

opts.text: HOLD or REDUCE caloric advancement at 800–1000 kcal/day until refeeding labs stabilize; resume advancement at 5–10 kcal/kg/day q2–3 days when stable
opts.rat: Standard refeeding approach. Labs guide advancement. Hold if deteriorating; resume gradually. The 'start low + go slow' principle. Target 30–40 kcal/kg/day for weight gain in chronic phase.

opts.text: Advance caloric intake rapidly to 2000 kcal/day immediately to achieve faster weight gain
opts.rat: ⚠️ DANGEROUSLY WRONG. Rapid advancement causes severe refeeding syndrome. Standard approach is start low + go slow. Target weight gain 0.5–1 kg/week. Faster gain = refeeding + fluid overload + cardiac strain.

opts.text: Aggressive IV fluid resuscitation with D5W to address bradycardia and hypotension
opts.rat: ⚠️ WRONG. (1) Adapted bradycardia + hypotension should NOT be aggressively corrected (chronic adaptation; CHF risk with refeeding fluid retention + cardiomyopathy). (2) D5W BEFORE thiamine = Wernicke encephalopathy precipitation. If IV fluid needed, use NS or LR; cautious volume.

opts.text: Continuous cardiac monitoring (telemetry); ECG every shift; echo to assess cardiomyopathy + pericardial effusion + mitral valve prolapse; cardiology consultation if concerning
opts.rat: Standard anorexia cardiac monitoring. Sudden death major mortality cause. Echo for structural assessment. Continuous tele x first week of refeeding. Avoid QT-prolonging medications.

opts.text: 1:1 sitter at bedside for safety + meal supervision (prevents food hiding/disposal/exercising + safety + companionship)
opts.rat: Standard safety + meal supervision. Anorexia patients may hide food, exercise covertly, induce vomiting. Sitter provides constant supervision + companionship + safety. 1:1 reduces during stabilization.

opts.text: Bupropion 150 mg PO daily for comorbid depression
opts.rat: ⚠️ CONTRAINDICATED in eating disorders due to SEIZURE RISK (low body weight + electrolyte derangement). FLUOXETINE preferred for comorbid depression/OCD in anorexia (limited benefit but best evidence). OLANZAPINE low-dose option for anxiety + small weight gain effect.

opts.text: Multidisciplinary team coordination: medical (refeeding management), psychiatry (eating disorder + comorbid mood/anxiety + SI), registered dietitian (meal plan + supervision), psychotherapy (CBT/DBT/FBT), social work (family + insurance + discharge planning)
opts.rat: Standard of care for anorexia. Each discipline essential. Long-term recovery requires sustained multidisciplinary support. FBT (family-based therapy) particularly effective in adolescents + young adults.

opts.text: FAMILY-BASED TREATMENT (FBT) involvement: parents at bedside; family meeting today with full team; education on FBT principles (parents take charge of meals + weight restoration early; gradual return of autonomy)
opts.rat: FBT (Maudsley model) is evidence-based + effective in young adults. Parents engaged in meal supervision continues outpatient. Family education + therapy. Mother's bulimia history relevant family dynamic to address.

opts.text: Daily weights at consistent time (gown only, after voiding, before breakfast — standardized to detect true changes vs fluid fluctuations)
opts.rat: Standard anorexia weight monitoring. Standardization prevents misleading fluctuations from clothes, fluid, food. Trend matters more than single value. Weight discussed with patient per institutional protocol (some recommend 'blind weights' for psychological reasons).

opts.text: Long-term care planning: transition to specialized eating disorder unit or intensive outpatient program (IOP) when medically stable; multidisciplinary follow-up; family engagement; recurrence prevention
opts.rat: Standard discharge planning. Medical stabilization first; psychiatric/eating disorder care continues at higher level (ED unit, IOP, then outpatient). Long-term recovery months-years; recurrence ~30%. Sustained multidisciplinary support.

steps.title: Take Action
steps.sub: What is the FIRST action sequence?
steps.inst: Rank the order in which the nurse should implement these actions. #1 = FIRST.

opts.text: STAT IV phosphate 15 mmol sodium phosphate over 4 hr + IV KCl 40 mEq over 4 hr + IV magnesium sulfate 2 g over 1 hr; continuous cardiac monitoring; recheck phos q6h, K + Mg q4h; oral supplements added
opts.rat: #1 — FIRST. Refeeding electrolytes are the imminent danger. IV replacement for severe deficiencies. Cardiac monitoring throughout. Lab trending guides further care.

opts.text: HOLD caloric advancement at 800–1000 kcal/day until labs stabilize; THIAMINE 200 mg IV daily continued (Wernicke prevention); strict 1:1 meal supervision continues (prevents food hiding/disposal)
opts.rat: #2 — Adjust refeeding + maintain prophylaxis + safety. Holding advancement prevents worsening refeeding. Thiamine continues. Meal supervision prevents covert restriction.

opts.text: 12-lead ECG q-shift; echo to assess cardiomyopathy + pericardial effusion + mitral valve prolapse; cardiology consultation for concerning findings; AVOID QT-prolonging medications
opts.rat: #3 — Cardiac assessment. Sudden death is major mortality. Echo at admission; serial ECGs. Avoid QT-prolonging medication combinations (already prolonged from electrolyte + chronic).

opts.text: MULTIDISCIPLINARY TEAM coordination: psychiatry + dietitian + psychotherapy + social work + family-based treatment (FBT); 1:1 sitter for safety + meal supervision; family meeting today with full team for FBT education + discharge planning + expectations
opts.rat: #4 — Comprehensive care + family. Multidisciplinary team is standard. FBT particularly effective in young adults. Family meeting essential for treatment plan alignment. Mother's bulimia history relevant family dynamic.

opts.text: Long-term care planning: transition to specialized eating disorder unit or IOP when medically stable; outpatient multidisciplinary follow-up; family engagement; medication consideration (fluoxetine for depression/OCD; AVOID bupropion); medical alert + recurrence prevention
opts.rat: #5 — Sustained recovery. Long-term recovery is months-years; recurrence common. Specialized eating disorder programs effective. Outpatient structure essential. Family + peer support resources. Lifelong vigilance for relapse.

steps.title: Evaluate Outcomes
steps.inst: For each finding, classify the trajectory.

opts.text: Day 4: Phos 2.6, K 4.0, Mg 1.9 (all stable in normal range after IV + oral replacement); QTc 440 (improved from 478); no new PVCs; nutrition advanced to 1200 kcal/day; patient reports feeling 'less weak'; cardiac monitoring continued
opts.rat: Excellent response to refeeding management. Electrolytes stable; cardiac findings improving. Continue advancement 5–10 kcal/kg/day q2–3 days. Continue monitoring + thiamine + 1:1 supervision. Engaged in psychotherapy + FBT.

opts.text: Day 10: BMI 14.5 (gaining 0.5 kg/week); electrolytes stable; QTc normal; engaging in psychotherapy + DBT skills; family meetings ongoing; planning transition to specialized eating disorder unit when discharged from medical floor in 2–3 days
opts.rat: Successful medical stabilization + early recovery. Slow steady weight gain. Continued multidisciplinary care. Transition to higher level of care (ED unit or IOP) for ongoing eating disorder treatment. Family engagement essential.

opts.text: 3-month outpatient follow-up: BMI 17.2 (continued gradual gain); engaging in CBT weekly + dietitian biweekly + psychiatrist monthly; family FBT ongoing; mood improved; back in college part-time; supportive family + peer group; no recurrence to date
opts.rat: Long-term recovery trajectory. Continued multidisciplinary care + family + peer support. Gradual return to normal life with continued vigilance. Recurrence rate ~30% — long-term follow-up essential. Continued psychotherapy + nutrition optimization.

opts.text: Day 3 evening: develops new dyspnea, crackles bilateral bases, JVP elevated, pitting edema lower extremities — refeeding CARDIOMYOPATHY + fluid overload (CHF); diuretic given; nutrition advancement HELD
opts.rat: Refeeding CHF. Fluid retention from sodium/water shifts + reduced cardiac function from chronic malnutrition + carbohydrate-driven insulin shifts = volume overload + cardiac strain. Furosemide for diuresis; hold nutrition advancement; cardiology consultation; echo if new findings.

opts.text: Day 5: develops new confusion + nystagmus + ataxia despite thiamine prophylaxis — possible WERNICKE ENCEPHALOPATHY; IV thiamine dose doubled to 500 mg q8h; neurology consultation
opts.rat: Wernicke encephalopathy can occur despite prophylaxis (suboptimal absorption, increased needs from refeeding metabolism). Higher-dose IV thiamine; neurology consultation. Treat early to prevent Korsakoff (largely irreversible). Document thiamine timing meticulously.

opts.text: Day 4: another nurse, unaware of refeeding protocol, increased nutrition to 1500 kcal overnight; Phos dropped to 1.0; new PVCs progressing to brief NSVT; emergent IV phosphate + KCl + magnesium + cardiology + hold nutrition
opts.rat: ⚠️ Iatrogenic refeeding crisis from rapid advancement. The 'start low + go slow' principle must be enforced. Education for all staff. Document near-miss. Hold nutrition + electrolyte replacement + cardiac monitoring. Reinforce protocol.

opts.text: Day 7: patient caught hiding food in napkin during meal; 1:1 sitter intervenes; intake monitoring intensified; therapy session focuses on resistance + ambivalence about recovery; family informed
opts.rat: Expected resistance in anorexia treatment. Food hiding common. 1:1 sitter prevents but doesn't 'cure' the underlying drive. Therapy addresses ambivalence + motivation. Family involvement in problem-solving. Document; continue care.

opts.text: Discharge day 14: transferred to specialized eating disorder residential program for continued treatment; medical stable; weight gain 1 kg over admission; psychotherapy + family + multidisciplinary structure established; outpatient follow-up plan
opts.rat: Successful transition. Medical stabilization complete; ongoing eating disorder treatment at specialized facility. Continued multidisciplinary care. Family engagement. Long-term recovery focus. Outpatient structure scheduled.

opts.text: Persistent amenorrhea + osteoporosis (DEXA T-score -2.4) at 6-month follow-up despite weight restoration to BMI 18; continued OB-GYN + endocrinology follow-up; consider OCPs after weight stabilized
opts.rat: Expected residual findings. Hypothalamic amenorrhea + osteoporosis can persist after weight restoration. OB-GYN for menstruation + fertility; endocrinology for osteoporosis. OCPs controversial (don't restore natural cycle but may help bone density); calcium + vit D + weight-bearing exercise; bisphosphonate generally avoided in young women.

opts.text: Family verbalize relief at multidisciplinary approach; mother shares her own bulimia history + recovery; family agrees to FBT principles + outpatient support; therapy includes family sessions; PCP coordinated
opts.rat: Family engagement essential. Mother's recovery experience can be both helpful (understanding) and challenging (family dynamics). Family therapy addresses both. FBT principles continue outpatient. Multigenerational eating disorder patterns sometimes present — address directly.
